network.overall_strategy: The network targets threonine by building a densely connected prebiotic graph around four hub intermediates: formate, formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and pyruvate, with acetaldehyde and aspartate-semialdehyde analog chemistry providing convergent entry to alpha- and beta-functionalized amino-acid precursors. Hydrothermal chemistry fixes CO2 into reduced C1-C3 compounds on iron-sulfur minerals; surface photochemistry and wet-dry reactions convert these into aldehydes, sugars, aminonitriles, and amino acids; biochemical/prebiotic steps then couple alpha-keto acid amination, transamination, aldol-type carbon-carbon formation, and Strecker-like additions to deliver threonine through multiple redundant routes. The 10 pathways share hubs and cross-environment transport, satisfying hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical interconnection.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface electron transfer reduces CO2 to surface-bound formyl equivalents that are released as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Iron-sulfur minerals are widely invoked as prebiotic catalysts for CO2 fixation under hydrothermal conditions; greigite and mackinawite plausibly mediate formate production in alkaline vents, consistent with vent geochemistry and FeS-world models (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Russell & Martin, 2004; Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Magnetite/iron reduction of formate to formaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Sequential mineral-mediated reduction of C1 carboxylate to a more reduced aldehyde pool.
reactions.reasoning: Reduced iron phases and magnetite can support progressive carbon reduction under hydrothermal conditions; direct formaldehyde accumulation is likely low and transient, so this node represents a reactive C1 aldehyde pool linking hydrothermal fixation to surface sugar chemistry (McCollom & Seewald, 2007; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type coupling to glycolaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Surface-mediated C-C coupling among reduced C1 species yields C2 oxygenates including glycolaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry can generate oxygenated C2 compounds from reduced carbon feeds on transition-metal mineral surfaces; glycolaldehyde is a plausible bridge from vent carbon fixation to surface carbohydrate chemistry (Cody et al., 2000; McCollom et al., 1999).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical generation of HCN

conditions.other: UV irradiation, evaporitic or volcanic pool, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven redox chemistry in mineral-rich surface settings generates nitrile-bearing C1 species and accumulates HCN in drying pools.
reactions.reasoning: HCN-centered prebiotic chemistry is strongly associated with UV-active surface environments and sulfide-enabled cyanosulfidic regimes; this step abstracts formation of the cyanide pool from reduced carbon and nitrogen inputs under photochemical conditions (Patel et al., 2015; Ritson & Sutherland, 2012).

reactions.name: Borate-stabilized formose entry to glyceraldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Aldol/formose chemistry converts formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde into triose sugars; borate stabilizes the resulting sugar pool.
reactions.reasoning: Formose-type chemistry can generate triose sugars from C1/C2 aldehydes, and borate minerals increase plausibility by stabilizing ribose-related sugar isomers and suppressing decomposition (Ricardo et al., 2004; Benner et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal synthesis of pyruvate from reduced carbon
reactions.mechanism: Successive mineral-catalyzed carbon fixation and C-C coupling generate a C3 alpha-keto acid pool represented by pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a recurring target in prebiotic protometabolic models because it links carbon fixation with amino acid synthesis; FeS/mineral catalysis under vent conditions offers a plausible route to alpha-keto acids (Muchowska et al., 2019; Varma et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker-type access to alanine from acetaldehyde and HCN
reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde forms an imine with ammonia, cyanide adds to form an aminonitrile, and subsequent hydrolysis yields alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Strecker synthesis is a canonical prebiotic route to alpha-amino acids from aldehydes, ammonia, and HCN, especially in evaporative settings where concentrations rise and hydrolysis occurs over time (Miller, 1957; Islam et al., 2017).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine
reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate is converted to an imine/iminium equivalent followed by reduction to alanine on iron-sulfur mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids is central to protometabolic amino acid synthesis and is especially plausible for pyruvate to alanine in reducing hydrothermal systems (Huber & Wachtershauser, 2006; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: thermal decarboxylation in concentrated pools or interfaces

reactions.mechanism: Thermal loss of CO2 from pyruvate generates acetaldehyde as a reactive C2 aldehyde feeder.
reactions.reasoning: Decarboxylation of pyruvate is a plausible thermal transformation that links alpha-keto acid chemistry to aldehyde pools, enabling Strecker and aldol chemistry relevant to threonine precursors; the exact prebiotic efficiency is uncertain, so this step is partially speculative but chemically necessary for network closure (de Duve, 1991; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Biochemical transamination and carboxylation entry to aspartate pool

conditions.other: mineral interface or concentrated pool, proto-metabolic network

reactions.mechanism: Coupled transamination/carboxylation chemistry converts pyruvate-centered carbon into a dicarboxylate amino-acid pool represented by aspartate.
reactions.reasoning: Aspartate is a key branch-point amino acid in metabolism and a plausible prebiotic network intermediate even if no single robust abiotic route is universally accepted; this node serves as a conservative network abstraction for oxaloacetate/aspartate chemistry feeding homoserine/threonine-like routes (Morowitz et al., 2000; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker-like synthesis of beta-functionalized aminonitrile precursor pool
reactions.mechanism: Aldehyde coupling followed by ammonia/cyanide addition generates a pool of beta-hydroxylated aminonitrile-like intermediates relevant to threonine skeleton assembly.
reactions.reasoning: This step is a network-level abstraction of plausible Strecker/aldol combinations that install both amino and beta-hydroxyl functionality on a C4 skeleton. Direct literature support for threonine-specific aminonitrile pools is weaker than for simpler amino acids, so this reaction is partially speculative but grounded in established Strecker and aldehyde-coupling chemistry (Miller, 1957; Powner et al., 2009).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic convergence to alpha-ketobutyrate analogue

conditions.other: geothermal cycling, concentration on mineral interfaces

reactions.mechanism: Carbon-carbon extension, redox adjustment, and partial hydrolysis/transamination condense multiple precursor pools into a C4 alpha-keto acid analogue represented by alpha-ketobutyrate.
reactions.reasoning: Alpha-ketobutyrate is the immediate keto-acid precursor to threonine-like amino acid chemistry in modern metabolism, so introducing it as a convergence node is chemically justified. The exact abiotic route remains open, but coupling aldehyde, nitrile, and dicarboxylate chemistry in concentrated prebiotic settings is a reasonable bridging hypothesis (de Duve, 1991; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic amination-hydroxylation to threonine

conditions.other: concentrated pool, repeated heating/cooling, no O2

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-ketobutyrate analogue undergoes amination and beta-hydroxyl incorporation via triose-derived chemistry to yield threonine.
reactions.reasoning: This final step represents the chemically necessary conversion of a C4 keto-acid precursor into beta-hydroxylated alpha-amino acid product. While a single experimentally established abiotic threonine synthesis route is not definitive, the transformation is consistent with prebiotic amination and carbonyl hydration/addition logic in mixed precursor pools (Lazcano & Miller, 1996; Islam et al., 2017).

pathways.name: Vent carbon fixation to keto-acid threonine route
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced hydrothermally to formate and pyruvate, then funneled through alpha-ketobutyrate analogue to threonine.

pathways.name: Vent pyruvate to alanine to aspartate-assisted threonine route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is aminated to alanine, enters an aspartate-like pool, then converges to alpha-ketobutyrate and threonine.

pathways.name: Formate-formaldehyde-glycolaldehyde sugar-assisted route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal C1 reduction generates formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde, which feed surface sugar chemistry before converging on threonine.

pathways.name: Surface HCN-Strecker alanine branch to threonine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formaldehyde seeds surface HCN generation; acetaldehyde then undergoes Strecker synthesis to alanine, feeding keto-acid convergence and threonine.

pathways.name: Pyruvate decarboxylation and aminonitrile route
pathways.description: Pyruvate is thermally decarboxylated to acetaldehyde; together with glycolaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN it forms a beta-functionalized aminonitrile pool that converges to threonine.

pathways.name: Sugar-first triose-assisted threonine route
pathways.description: Reduced C1 species form glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde on the surface; trioses then assist the final hydroxylated amino-acid assembly.

pathways.name: HCN-centered route via dual surface inputs
pathways.description: Surface HCN generated from hydrothermal formaldehyde combines with pyruvate-derived acetaldehyde and glycolaldehyde to produce aminonitrile precursors to threonine.

pathways.name: Aspartate-branch convergence route
pathways.description: Alanine and pyruvate generate an aspartate-like branch node that converges with aldehyde chemistry to the threonine precursor.

pathways.name: Dual alanine-source route to threonine
pathways.description: Alanine is accessible both hydrothermally and by surface Strecker chemistry, providing redundant entry into later convergence chemistry en route to threonine.

pathways.name: Maximum interconnection hydrothermal-surface-biochemical route
pathways.description: This pathway traverses all three environments: vent CO2 reduction, surface HCN and sugar chemistry, biochemical aldehyde/keto-acid convergence, and final threonine formation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong hydrothermal start through formate formation, but the route becomes weak at the formaldehyde-to-glycolaldehyde coupling step and especially at the final conversion of an alpha-ketobutyrate analogue plus ammonia and glyceraldehyde directly to threonine. The target is nominally reached, but the end chemistry is not well grounded.
suggestions: Replace rxn_013 with a more plausible sequence via alpha-ketobutyrate amination to aminobutyrate followed by hydroxylation or via a beta-hydroxy alpha-keto acid precursor
Clarify the chemical identity of mol_012 and make downstream chemistry match that identity
Provide a better-supported route for glycolaldehyde formation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Pyruvate-to-alanine by reductive amination is one of the stronger elements in the network, but the proposed transamination/carboxylation entry to aspartate is chemically underspecified, and the convergence to alpha-ketobutyrate analogue is too compressed. Final threonine formation remains the main weakness.
suggestions: Separate aspartate formation into explicit oxaloacetate formation and transamination steps
Introduce a realistic beta-hydroxy precursor before threonine
Avoid combining multiple bond-forming events into a single biochemical reaction node

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The hydrothermal-to-surface transition is conceptually good, and borate-stabilized sugar chemistry is plausible. However, the network overstates confidence in hydrothermal glycolaldehyde production and still terminates in an implausible threonine-forming step.
suggestions: Use surface formose/aldol chemistry rather than hydrothermal FTT-like coupling for C2 sugar formation
Connect glyceraldehyde to a better-defined beta-hydroxy amino acid precursor

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the weakest routes. Surface photochemical generation of HCN directly from formaldehyde and ammonia is not a standard prebiotic route, and Strecker synthesis from acetaldehyde would more naturally yield an alanine aminonitrile/hydrolysis sequence, not alanine directly in one step. Downstream convergence remains highly speculative.
suggestions: Treat HCN as an exogenous atmospheric/input species or derive it from more plausible photochemical nitrogen-carbon chemistry
Split Strecker chemistry into aminonitrile formation and hydrolysis
Rebuild the route around experimentally supported aminonitrile intermediates

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Pyruvate decarboxylation to acetaldehyde is reasonable, but the proposed beta-functionalized aminonitrile pool from acetaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN is too vaguely specified to be evaluated as a discrete product. This ambiguity propagates into the alpha-ketobutyrate analogue and target steps.
suggestions: Define mol_015 structurally as a specific aminonitrile
Use explicit aldol and Strecker substeps for beta-functionalized precursor construction
Add hydrolysis or transamination steps to connect aminonitrile chemistry to amino acids

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The sugar-first logic is coherent as a topological idea, but the chemistry from sugars to threonine precursor is compressed and unsupported. Glyceraldehyde is plausible; conversion of mixed sugar/aldehyde/aminonitrile pools into a defined threonine precursor is not.
suggestions: Introduce a defined beta-hydroxy alpha-keto acid intermediate
Avoid generic pool species where a target-specific route is needed
Support sugar-to-amino-acid transition with explicit carbon-carbon and nitrogen-installation steps

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This HCN-centered route depends on the least convincing parts of the network. Both HCN generation and downstream aminonitrile pooling are poorly specified, making the overall path low confidence despite acceptable environmental staging.
suggestions: Anchor HCN chemistry in better-supported cyanosulfidic or atmospheric delivery scenarios
Use specific aminonitriles and hydrolysis products rather than abstract precursor pools
Redesign the late-stage target-forming chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This branch benefits from a plausible alanine-forming step but is undermined by an overly compressed aspartate branch and a poorly defined convergence into mol_012. The route does not convincingly achieve threonine despite nominal graph connectivity.
suggestions: Model oxaloacetate or a credible prebiotic equivalent explicitly
Separate carbon skeleton extension from amination/hydroxylation chemistry
Use known alpha-keto/beta-hydroxy acid relationships to approach threonine

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Dual sourcing of alanine improves redundancy, but one source is weak Strecker shorthand and the downstream route to threonine is still not chemically secured. Good topological redundancy does not rescue low mechanistic confidence.
suggestions: Keep pyruvate reductive amination as the primary alanine source
Represent Strecker-derived alanine via aminonitrile and hydrolysis explicitly
Redesign late-stage threonine assembly around stepwise transformations

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the best overall route because it captures the intended hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical flow and uses the strongest early reactions in the network. However, the network still fails at the crucial target-forming endgame: mol_015 and mol_012 are insufficiently defined, and the final transformation to threonine is not chemically convincing.
suggestions: Retain the early hydrothermal C1-C3 chemistry but replace the late-stage assembly with a defined sequence through beta-hydroxy alpha-keto acid or aminonitrile intermediates
Specify explicit structures for mol_012 and mol_015
Add literature-backed steps for hydrolysis, transamination, and stereochemically agnostic beta-hydroxylation

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically strong but chemically uneven. It satisfies the requested graph density well: 15 molecule nodes and 13 reaction nodes provide a connected network with multiple hubs and convergent branches. Environmental interconnection is also a strength, with clear hydrothermal generation of reduced carbon, surface sugar/HCN chemistry, and biochemical consolidation. However, target-directed chemistry is weak. The most serious issues are: (1) implausible or underspecified HCN generation from formaldehyde and ammonia, (2) shorthand Strecker reactions that collapse aminonitrile formation and hydrolysis into a single step, (3) vague pooled species such as the aminonitrile precursor pool and alpha-ketobutyrate analogue, and (4) an unconvincing final step from mol_012 + NH3 + glyceraldehyde to threonine. Several early hydrothermal steps are plausible in broad outline, especially CO2 to formate, pyruvate formation, pyruvate decarboxylation, and reductive amination to alanine, but the network does not yet demonstrate a chemically credible final route to threonine. Mineral-agent compliance cannot be fully verified from the summary, but the named hydrothermal and surface reactions at least imply appropriate mineral contexts. Overall, this is a good connectivity design but not an approved synthesis network for threonine.
